name: Where Ironheart Rests
description: Kell has the full shape of what Kaelen built. What he doesn't have is the hammer itself — or who has been keeping it. Osse said it needs to be findable. She said it to you specifically.
accept_text: Kell has been quiet since you returned from the tomb. When you find him he's at the monastery window with the Elder fragment in his hands, looking east at nothing. 'She said it needs to be findable,' he says without turning. 'She said that to you. Not to me — I've asked her questions for twenty years and she has never said anything like that to me.' He finally turns. 'Osse speaks precisely. She always has. She is a woman who has spent thirty years maintaining exact language because imprecision in an oral history means the history changes.' He sets the fragment down on the windowsill. 'She told you the hammer needs to be findable. That is not an observation. That is a statement about who should find it.' He meets your eyes. 'She's been keeping it. She has been in that tomb for thirty years, with a dead man and a warm hammer, waiting for the person she was going to say that sentence to.' He looks at the fragment. 'Go back to her. Tell her Kell says: his answer to her question is yes. She will know what that means — we have been corresponding about the question of whether the hammer is a thing or a choice for eleven years. She'll know I've resolved it.' A pause. 'I haven't. But I think you have. And I think that's what she's been waiting to find out.'
complete_text: Osse stands when you deliver the message. She goes to the alcove beside the bier — the one with the old stone shelf you didn't notice the first time, the stone smooth from thirty years of someone's hand resting on it daily. She opens a case that predates her by centuries: cold-sealed metal, an Elder lock mechanism, the kind that responds to the correct pressure rather than a key. Inside: a hammer. Short-hafted, worn grip, the head a dark alloy with no current name. And warm. Not fever-warm. The specific warmth of a hand recently removed from the grip. Osse does not touch it. 'He held it at the siege gate for six hours,' she says. 'The decision not to swing is in the metal. It has been there for eleven hundred years.' She looks at you for a long time — the assessment that Rememberers make, the one that is not judgment but inventory, cataloguing what you've seen and understood and carried to this moment. 'You've been to the Spire. You've spoken with the Architect. You've found what the Ninth knows.' She closes the case and places it in your hands. It is heavier than it looks and the warmth comes through the case walls. 'Take it to Vespa. She said yes first. She should carry it.' A pause. 'She'll be at the market. She's almost always at the market.' She sits back down in her corner, lamp at the precise angle of thirty years of occupation. 'Tell her the record has a new chapter. Tell her I've never added a chapter before. She'll understand what that costs.'
